title: Foreign
subtitle: World cinema survey — French, Italian, Japanese, Scandinavian, Latin American, Korean, Eastern European starting points.

sections.name: I. French
sections.desc: From Renoir through New Wave to modern.

items.title: The Rules of the Game
items.whyPriority: Routinely listed among the greatest films ever made. Sight & Sound poll mainstay. Country house weekend among the French aristocracy on the eve of WWII. Devastating despite the comedy.
items.pitch: Aristocrats and servants gather at a country estate. Affairs, hunts, secrets, deaths. Pre-war French society laid bare.

critics.quote: #11 in Greatest Films of All Time poll.

items.title: The 400 Blows
items.whyPriority: French New Wave's most accessible founding text. Truffaut's autobiographical first feature. The final freeze-frame is among cinema's greatest endings.
items.pitch: A neglected Parisian boy of about 12 drifts toward delinquency. Truffaut's debut.

critics.quote: Won Best Director.

items.title: Breathless
items.whyPriority: The other founding New Wave text. Jump cuts, handheld, jazz. The film that showed cinema didn't have to follow the rules.
items.pitch: A small-time French criminal kills a cop and tries to convince his American girlfriend to flee with him.

critics.quote: Berlin Silver Bear. Routinely cited as one of the most influential films ever made.

items.title: A Prophet
items.whyPriority: Cross-listed from heist. Modern French masterpiece.
items.pitch: Six years in a French prison. Crime as education.

critics.quote: Grand Prix.

items.title: Le Samouraï
items.whyPriority: Alain Delon as a near-silent hitman. Minimalism, ritual, blue-grey Paris. Influence on Tarantino, Mann, Refn directly stated.
items.pitch: A hitman kills a club owner. Witnesses give conflicting descriptions. The police and his employers begin to close in.

critics.quote: Routinely cited among the greatest crime films. Sight & Sound canonical.

items.title: Bicycle Thieves
items.whyPriority: Italian neo-realism's masterpiece. Postwar Rome. A father and son search for a stolen bicycle. Devastating in its smallness.
items.pitch: A man finally lands a job that requires a bicycle. His bicycle is stolen on the first day. He and his son search the city.

critics.quote: Routinely cited among the greatest films ever made. Sight & Sound canonical.

items.title: 8½
items.whyPriority: Cross-listed with Fellini auteur. The film about being unable to make a film. Mastroianni as Fellini's stand-in. Routinely top-10 of all time.
items.pitch: A film director suffers creative block while preparing his next picture. His memories, dreams, and women converge.

critics.quote: Top 10 in critics' polls historically.

items.title: L'Avventura
items.whyPriority: A woman disappears on a Mediterranean island. Her boyfriend and best friend search but the search becomes something else. The film that proved cinema didn't need narrative resolution.
items.pitch: A yacht trip. A woman disappears. Nobody finds her. Or seems particularly to mind.

critics.quote: Booed at Cannes; immediately rehabilitated. Sight & Sound canonical.

items.title: Cinema Paradiso
items.whyPriority: A boy and a small-town projectionist. Adult/young versions. The 'kissing scene' montage is one of cinema's beloved moments. Unabashedly emotional.
items.pitch: A successful filmmaker returns home to Sicily upon hearing of an old friend's death.

critics.quote: Won Best Foreign Language Film.

items.title: Il Postino
items.pitch: A postman in 1950s Italy delivers mail to exiled Pablo Neruda. They become friends. Massimo Troisi died days after filming.

critics.quote: Five Oscar nominations including Best Picture.

sections.name: III. Japanese
sections.desc: Cross-listed heavily with Kurosawa auteur tracker.

items.title: Tokyo Story
items.whyPriority: Often named among the greatest films ever made. Aging parents visit their adult children in Tokyo. The children are too busy to give them time. Quiet, devastating.
items.pitch: An elderly couple travel from rural Japan to Tokyo to see their grown children, who don't have time for them.

critics.quote: Voted #1 by directors in the Greatest Films of All Time poll.

items.title: Seven Samurai
items.whyPriority: Kurosawa's biggest film. The template for every 'team gathered to defend a town' story since. Cross-listed with Kurosawa auteur.
items.pitch: A 16th-century Japanese village hires seven masterless samurai to defend them against bandits.

critics.quote: Routinely top of Japanese-cinema and world-cinema lists.

items.title: Rashomon
items.pitch: Cross-listed with Kurosawa auteur. The film that introduced Japanese cinema to the West.

critics.quote: Won Golden Lion at Venice. Honorary Oscar for Best Foreign Language Film.

items.title: Ugetsu
items.whyPriority: Mizoguchi's ghost story. 16th century, two peasants pursue ambition during civil war. Visually transcendent.
items.pitch: Two peasants leave their village during a war for fortune and military glory. Their wives suffer the consequences.

critics.quote: Won Silver Lion at Venice. Mizoguchi widely considered one of cinema's greatest stylists.

items.title: Spirited Away
items.whyPriority: You said no anime, but Miyazaki occupies different territory than what most people mean by anime — and Spirited Away regularly tops greatest-of-the-21st-century lists. Worth flagging in case the word 'anime' was the issue, not the work.
items.pitch: A 10-year-old girl wanders into a spirit world. To save her parents, she must work in a bathhouse for the gods.

critics.quote: Won Best Animated Feature. Routinely top of best-of-21st-century polls.

items.title: The Seventh Seal
items.whyPriority: Cross-listed with Bergman auteur. The chess game with Death. Among the most parodied images in cinema, but the film itself is profound.
items.pitch: A knight returns from the Crusades to plague-ravaged Sweden and plays chess with Death.

critics.quote: Special Jury Prize.

items.title: Wild Strawberries
items.whyPriority: Cross-listed with Bergman auteur. An old professor confronts his life. Bergman's most accessible.
items.pitch: An aging Swedish professor drives to receive an honorary degree. The journey becomes a confrontation with his past.

critics.quote: Berlin Golden Bear. Routinely cited as Bergman's most accessible great film.

items.title: Songs from the Second Floor
items.whyPriority: Andersson's deadpan absurdist style. Tableaux of human desolation that are also somehow funny. Like nothing else in cinema.
items.pitch: A loosely connected series of static-camera scenes about modern alienation, played as dark comedy.

critics.quote: Jury Prize. Andersson's international breakthrough.

items.title: The Hunt
items.whyPriority: Mads Mikkelsen as a kindergarten teacher falsely accused of abuse. Slow-build mob horror in a small town. Devastating.
items.pitch: A village rallies against a man one of its children has accused. The child cannot recant; the village will not listen.

critics.quote: Mikkelsen won Best Actor.

sections.name: V. Latin American
sections.desc: The new wave that produced Cuarón, del Toro, Iñárritu.

items.title: City of God
items.whyPriority: Brazilian masterpiece. Decades of life in a Rio favela. Kinetic, structurally inventive, devastating.
items.pitch: A young man tries to escape gang life in 1970s Rio while his peers fall deeper in.

critics.quote: Four Oscar nominations including Best Director, Best Adapted Screenplay.

items.title: Y Tu Mamá También
items.whyPriority: Pre-Hollywood Cuarón. Two teenage boys take a road trip with an older woman. Mexico as character. The narration is one of the great choices in 21st-century cinema.
items.pitch: Two upper-middle-class Mexican teenagers and a woman in her late 20s take a beach road trip. Mexico is dying around them.

critics.quote: Two Oscar nominations including Best Original Screenplay.

items.title: The Motorcycle Diaries
items.pitch: Young Che Guevara's pre-revolutionary South American road trip. Gael García Bernal.

critics.quote: Won Best Original Song. Two nominations.

items.title: Roma
items.whyPriority: Cuarón's autobiographical black-and-white masterpiece. A family's domestic worker in 1970s Mexico City. Won Best Foreign Language Film + Best Director + Best Cinematography.
items.pitch: A year in the life of a middle-class Mexican family and their indigenous housekeeper.

critics.quote: Won 3 Oscars. Ten total nominations including Best Picture.

sections.name: VI. Korean Modern
sections.desc: The most consistent national cinema of the 21st century.

items.title: Parasite
items.whyPriority: Won Best Picture. The first non-English-language film to do so. Class-warfare thriller that pivots multiple times. If you've somehow missed it.
items.pitch: A poor family schemes their way into employment with a wealthy family. Things escalate.

critics.quote: Won 4 Oscars including Best Picture.

items.title: Memories of Murder
items.pitch: Cross-listed from crime. Bong's pre-Parasite masterpiece.

items.title: Andrei Rublev
items.whyPriority: Cross-listed with Tarkovsky auteur. Three-and-a-half hours about a 15th-century Russian icon painter. Spiritual, brutal, beautiful. Tarkovsky's masterpiece.
items.pitch: Episodes from the life of medieval icon painter Andrei Rublev. Russian feudalism, faith, art.

critics.quote: Routinely listed among the greatest films ever made. Sight & Sound canonical.

items.title: Three Colors trilogy: Blue, White, Red
items.whyPriority: Polish-French. Three films loosely on liberty, equality, fraternity. Three Colors: Red is generally considered the masterpiece. Watch in order or just watch Red.
items.pitch: Three thematically connected films about loss, justice, and connection in 1990s Europe.

critics.quote: Red received 3 Oscar nominations including Best Director — extraordinary for a foreign-language film.

items.title: Sátántangó
items.whyPriority: Seven-and-a-half hours, black-and-white, Hungarian collective farm in collapse. Tarr's most extreme film. Either a mountain to climb or genuinely transcendent.
items.pitch: Late communism. A failed collective farm. The arrival of a charismatic con man.

critics.quote: Routinely cited as a high point of slow cinema. Susan Sontag wrote that she'd be willing to see it once a year for the rest of her life.
